coredata.dc:title: Functional carbon nanodots enhance tomato tolerance to zinc deficient soils: Mechanisms and structure-function relationships

coredata.dc:description: Zinc (Zn) deficiency is a global problem disorder affecting both crops and humans. Herein, modified functional carbon nanodots (MFCNs) with various structures and characteristics were developed to regulate tomato yields and Zn migration in plant–soil systems affected by Zn deficiency through structure–function relationships. Sulfur-doped FCNs (S-FCNs), nitrogen-doped FCNs (N-FCNs), and nitrogen‑sulfur co-doped FCNs (N,S-FCNs) were hydrothermally modified using FCNs as precursors. Their regulatory effects on tomatoes growing in Zn-deficient alkaline soils were studied in pot culture experiments. Specifically, 8 mg kg−1 of FCNs and S-FCNs improved tomato yields by 132 % and 108 %, respectively, compared with the control. However, N-FCNs and N,S-FCNs showed no significant effect on yield compared with the control (P < 0.05). Moreover, the application of FCNs or S-FCNs significantly improved fruit quality and nutritional value, including Zn content (by 26.3 % and 22.0 %, respectively) and naturally occurring antioxidants (by 3.37- and 2.08-fold for lycopene, 1.31- and 1.18-fold for flavonoids, and 2.28- and 1.89-fold for phenolics, respectively; P < 0.05). Although N-FCNs and N,S-FCNs increased Zn contents, they inhibited the synthesis of naturally occurring antioxidants in fruits. Zn bioaccessibility, uptake, and transportation in plant-soil systems were regulated by MFCNs through both direct and indirect mechanisms, including ionic reactions, plant physiology, and environmental effects. MFCNs regulated plant tolerance to Zn deficiency not only by affecting root activity, redox homeostasis, micronutrient balance, chelator synthesis, genetic expression, and plant photosynthesis but also by influencing rhizosphere soil properties and the microbial environment. Based on their dual role as “plant growth regulators” and “soil conditioners”, MFCNs may have general applicability in agriculture. This study highlights the behavior of MFCNs in plant-soil systems, providing innovative nanotools for enhancing Zn availability, crop stress resistance and environmental preservation in sustainable agriculture.

Effects of MFCNs on tomato growth, yield, and fruit quality. a, DWs of tomato plants on the 25th day. b, Relative fruit yield of tomato plants in each group. c, Zn content in fruit (N = 3). d–f, Contents of lycopene, phenolics, and flavonoids in tomato fruits grown under Zn deficiency. Data are reported as mean ± SD (N = 4) except Zn content. Different lowercase and uppercase letters above bars indicate significant differences among treatments at P < 0.05 under Zn deficiency and normal Zn conditions, respectively.

Effects of MFCNs on Zn and other ionome in plant-soil systems. a–b, Zn BCF and TF in each treatment. c, Zn contents in roots and leaves. d, Available Zn concentration in rhizosphere soils. e–f, Content of nutrient elements in plant leaves. Data from three experiments are reported as the mean ± SD. Different lowercase letters indicate significant differences among treatments at P < 0.05.

Effect of various MFCNs on plant root activity (a), root PRO content (b), Glu content (c), root total acids content (d), and leaf Chl level (e). Data from four experiments (N = 4) are reported as mean ± SD. Different lowercase and uppercase letters above bars indicate significant differences among treatments at P < 0.05 under Zn deficiency and normal Zn conditions, respectively.

Effects of various MFCNs on antioxidants and membrane stability in tomato plants. a–c, SOD, CAT and POD activities. d, GSH level. e, MDA content. f, Relative membrane permeability of leaves. Data from four independent experiments (N = 4) are reported as mean ± SD. Different lowercase and uppercase letters above bars indicate significant differences among treatments at P < 0.05 under Zn deficiency and normal Zn conditions, respectively.

Effects of various MFCNs on the enzymatic activity of URE (a), ALP (b), and SC (c) in rhizosphere soils. Data from four experiments (N = 4) are reported as mean ± SD. Different lowercase letters indicate significant differences among treatments at P < 0.05.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zinc (Zn) deficiency is a global problem disorder affecting both crops and humans. Herein, modified functional carbon nanodots (MFCNs) with various structures and characteristics were developed to regulate tomato yields and Zn migration in plant–soil systems affected by Zn deficiency through structure–function relationships. Sulfur-doped FCNs (S-FCNs), nitrogen-doped FCNs (N-FCNs), and nitrogen‑sulfur co-doped FCNs (N,S-FCNs) were hydrothermally modified using FCNs as precursors. Their regulatory effects on tomatoes growing in Zn-deficient alkaline soils were studied in pot culture experiments. Specifically, 8 mg kg−1 of FCNs and S-FCNs improved tomato yields by 132 % and 108 %, respectively, compared with the control. However, N-FCNs and N,S-FCNs showed no significant effect on yield compared with the control (P < 0.05). Moreover, the application of FCNs or S-FCNs significantly improved fruit quality and nutritional value, including Zn content (by 26.3 % and 22.0 %, respectively) and naturally occurring antioxidants (by 3.37- and 2.08-fold for lycopene, 1.31- and 1.18-fold for flavonoids, and 2.28- and 1.89-fold for phenolics, respectively; P < 0.05). Although N-FCNs and N,S-FCNs increased Zn contents, they inhibited the synthesis of naturally occurring antioxidants in fruits. Zn bioaccessibility, uptake, and transportation in plant-soil systems were regulated by MFCNs through both direct and indirect mechanisms, including ionic reactions, plant physiology, and environmental effects. MFCNs regulated plant tolerance to Zn deficiency not only by affecting root activity, redox homeostasis, micronutrient balance, chelator synthesis, genetic expression, and plant photosynthesis but also by influencing rhizosphere soil properties and the microbial environment. Based on their dual role as “plant growth regulators” and “soil conditioners”, MFCNs may have general applicability in agriculture. This study highlights the behavior of MFCNs in plant-soil systems, providing innovative nanotools for enhancing Zn availability, crop stress resistance and environmental preservation in sustainable agriculture.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Zinc (Zn) malnutrition is a global issue affecting over 2 billion people worldwide. Zn is an essential micronutrient for all organisms, including humans, and needs to be fortified in crop-based food products (Liu et al., 2023; Narayanan et al., 2019). Among soil and plant nutrient deficiencies, Zn deficiency is one of the most widespread limiting factors in crop production (Abadi et al., 2021). Although conventional fertilizers such as Zn-EDTA, Zn-diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (Zn-DTPA), and ZnSO4 are extensively applied to mitigate Zn deficiency and improve crop output, they are not very efficient due to high loss rates or adsorption by soil colloids, particularly in calcareous or alkaline soils (Baddar and Unrine, 2021; Read et al., 2021). Additionally, the excessive application of Zn fertilizers has led to soil degradation and water pollution (Savci, 2012). Thus, sustainable agriculture requires more environmentally friendly and economical approaches to address Zn deficiency in crops (Alikhani et al., 2023).

Zn uptake in crop tissues is positively correlated with the plant-available Zn in soils (Sun et al., 2023). Agronomic fortification of grains requires both the fertilization of Zn-deficient (ZD) soils and the development of methods to increase the bioavailability of insoluble Zn in soil (Saleem et al., 2023). Moreover, effective Zn absorption and redistribution in different plant organs are crucial factors affecting plant growth (Liu et al., 2023). Therefore, implementing effective approaches to increase nutrient absorbability and improve Zn bioaccessibility in soils is key to enhancing fertilizer efficiency and reducing fertilizer input.

Nanotechnology is attracting significant attention in agriculture owing to its potential to enhance crop production, improve fertilizer effectiveness, reduce nutrient loss, mitigate environmental issues, and enhance the productivity and safety of agricultural technologies (Zhang et al., 2021). Nanomaterials (NMs) such as Fe2O3 nanoparticles (NPs) (Cao et al., 2022c), ZnO NPs (Sun et al., 2023), carbon dots (CDs) (Alikhani et al., 2023), carbon nanotubes (Chandel et al., 2022), and fullerenes (Jha and Yadav, 2023), have been studied in relation to nanofertilizer development, fertilizer input reduction, and sustainable agricultural production. Particularly, quantum-sized CDs are advantageous because of their simple synthesis and surface modification, superior water solubility, fluorescence, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility (Guo et al., 2022b). At appropriate doses, CDs positively impact crop plant development and resistance to environmental stresses owing to their excellent performances as plant growth regulators and soil conditioners (Guo et al., 2022a, 2022b; Q. Chen et al., 2022b). Our previous studies demonstrated that at doses as low as 3–16 mg kg−1, environmentally friendly functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) can universally promote plant growth and increase crop yields by regulating plant physiological processes and soil properties. Specifically, FCNs can efficiently regulate the competitive absorption and reallocation of divalent metal cations, such as Fe2+, Pb2+, and Cd2+, in plants (Cao et al., 2024; Chen et al., 2021a). They also enhanced crop stress tolerance by improving plant physiology in arid and saline–alkali soils (Q. Chen et al., 2022a, 2022b). Additionally, FCNs improved soil physicochemical properties, nutrient cycling, and microbial structure, thereby improving crop tolerance and productivity (Q. Chen et al., 2022a, 2022b). Furthermore, an ecotoxicological evaluation of FCNs on zebrafish confirmed their biosecurity in animals (Chen et al., 2023). Thus, the main advantages of FCNs in agriculture include their small dosage, high efficiency, and low toxicity. Therefore, FCNs are promising nanotools for enhancing Zn fertilizer efficiency, reducing fertilizer input, and protecting the environment.

However, how the structure–function relationships of carbon-based NMs affect the fate of Zn in plant–soil systems—including Zn uptake, transport and storage—and the potential toxicity of various NMs following application remain unclear. Compared with undoped CDs, nitrogen doping enhances light conversion and electron supply during photosynthesis by altering the CDs structure (Wang et al., 2021). CDs induce reactive oxygen species (ROS)–independent Ca2+ mobilization in plants due to the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on their surfaces (Li et al., 2022). Additionally, our prior study demonstrated that the carboxyl-rich surface modification of FCNs was primarily responsible for their active redox characteristics, which in turn influenced plant responses and adaptations to biotic and abiotic stresses (Guo et al., 2023). Furthermore, FCNs rich in carboxyl and hydroxy groups increased the bioavailability of Cd2+ and Pb2+ in aquatic environments compared with nitrogen-doped FCNs (N-FCNs) modified with -NH2 and NH4 + (Chen et al., 2021a). Because slight differences in NMs structure can produce significantly different results in the environment (Cao et al., 2022b), comprehensive research is necessary to understand the behavior and fate of various modified FCNs (MFCNs) and their interactions with Zn fertilizers in plant–soil systems.

Tomato (Solanum lycopersicum L.) is a highly valued vegetable with low tolerance to Zn deficiency, and its global production is significantly impacted by Zn deficiency (Akther et al., 2020). Alkaline soils are particularly ZD because the bioavailability of most metals and nutrients, including Zn, is limited under alkaline conditions (Baddar and Unrine, 2021). Therefore, this study aimed to explore the role of MFCNs in a “tomato plant–alkali soil” system under Zn limitation as well as to elucidate their structure–function relationships and the mechanisms by which MFCNs regulate plant tolerance to Zn shortage in alkaline soils. To achieve this, parameters related to tomato plant growth, oxidative stress, osmotic adjustment, and gene expression were examined throughout the entire crop life cycle. Additionally, the effects of various MFCNs on the physicochemical properties and microbial structure of rhizosphere soils were characterized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Preparation and characterization of MFCNs
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, MFCNs refer to the FCN-based NMs modified with various surface functional groups, including FCNs, N-FCNs, S-FCNs, and N,S-FCNs. FCNs were synthesized using only graphite and water through our previously reported electrolytic method (Chen et al., 2020a). Ammonia, sodium sulfite, and thiourea were used as sources of N and/or sulfur (S) for the hydrothermal modification of FCNs to synthesize N-FCNs, S-FCNs, and N,S-FCNs, respectively. MFCNs were characterized using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL-1011, Japan), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Bruker Tensor 37, Germany), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, USA) and Malvern laser particle size analyzer (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90, UK). More detailed information on the synthesis and characterization methods can be found in the supplementary material (SM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental design and growth conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Because Zn deficiency is one of the most common nutritional disorders affecting crops grown in alkaline and sandy soils, this study used alkaline sandy soils as samples for ZD treatment (Shafigh et al., 2019). The alkaline sandy soil was sampled from a farmland in northern China. The initial properties of the soil were as follows: pH of 8.39, Zn content of 23 mg kg−1, soil organic matter (SOM) content of 3.07 g kg−1, available nitrogen (AN) content of 11.5 mg kg−1, available phosphate (AP) content of 8.5 mg kg−1, available potassium (AK) content of 48.4 mg kg−1, and available Zn (DTPA-extractable-Zn [DTPA-Zn] form) content of 0.37 mg kg−1. Based on the criteria established by Natasha et al. (2022) and Y. Chen et al. (2022), this soil was categorized as ZD for plant growth. A 1/20 Hoagland's nutrient solution (Coolaber Science & Technology Co., LTD, China; composition provided in the SM) with or without Zn was added to the soils to supply other essential plant nutrients. Soils containing 23 mg kg−1 Zn were used for ZD treatment, whereas those containing 53 mg kg−1 Zn were used for Zn normal (ZN) treatment. Two kilograms of air-dried soil were placed into plastic pots with a diameter of 15 cm, maintaining a soil depth of about 20 cm. Uniformly sized (11 ± 1 cm) 4-week-old tomato (Micro Tom) seedlings were transplanted into the pots and cultivated in a greenhouse in Jinan, Shandong Province (from October 2021 to November 2021) at a constant temperature of 25 °C, with a 16/8 h light/dark cycle and 75 % relative humidity. After transplanting, MFCNs stored at 4 °C were diluted to 50 mL and watered directly onto the tomato seedlings at a concentration of 8 mg kg−1 in the soils. Treatments in ZD or ZN soils were grouped based on the added NMs and abbreviated as ZDCT, ZDF, ZDN, ZDS, ZDNS, ZNCT, ZNF, ZNN, ZNS and ZNNS (Table S1). Tomato plants were watered every 5 days with 200 mL of dechlorinated tap water. Each pot was arranged in a completely randomized block design, with four replicates per treatment and weekly plant phenotyping records. After 40 days of cultivation, plant and soil samples were collected and prepared for further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of growth and physiological parameters
ce:section.ce:para: After harvest, the tomato yield and plant height were determined and recorded. The fresh plant was separated into different parts after carefully washed with tap water and deionized water. To avoid interference from the asynchronous fruit-bearing stage in each treatment, the dry weight (DW) of both the aerial parts and roots of the tomato plants was measured on the 25th day of cultivation. Chlorophyll (Chl) in the leaves was extracted with ethanol and quantified spectrophotometrically at 652 nm using an ultraviolet and visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis, Thermo Evolution 220, USA; Turner, 1981). Glucose (Glu) and proline (PRO) contents were measured via UV spectroscopy (Eliaspour et al., 2020; Xiao et al., 2020). Ascorbic acid (AsA) content was determined with fast blue salt B at 420 nm via UV spectroscopy (Zhang and Huang, 1993). Lycopene content in fresh tomatoes was quantified through colorimetry. Flavonoid and phenolic contents in dry fruits were estimated via the Al3+ complex method and tungsten–molybdate reduction method, respectively, using commercial kits (Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd., China). The activities of enzymes such as peroxidase (POD), superoxide dismutase (SOD), and catalase (CAT) were measured via UV spectroscopy using reagent kits (Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd., China). Malondialdehyde (MDA) and reduced glutathione (GSH) contents were determined using the thiobarbituric acid and dithionitrobenzoic acid methods, respectively (Ellman, 1959; Heath and Packer, 1968; Cao et al., 2022a). Total acid content was were determined via titration as indicated in the SM. The content of metal nutrients, including Zn, K, Mg, Ca, Fe, Cu, and Mn, in digested dry samples was determined using an inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (Agilent 5110, USA). The bioconcentration factor (BCF) and translocation factor (TF) were calculated based on Zn contents in the plant and soil. Relative gene expression levels of heavy metal ATPase 2 (HMA2) and heavy metal ATPase 3 (HMA3) were analyzed using RT-qPCR (primers listed in Table S2; more details are provided in the SM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of rhizosphere soil physicochemical properties and microbial community
ce:section.ce:para: The rhizosphere soil sample was collected by shaking the soil off the roots. The soil pH (soil/water, 1:5) was measured using a pH meter. SOM content was determined volumetrically using potassium dichromate (Osman et al., 2013). AN content was determined using the alkali diffusion method (Bao, 2005), AP content was analyzed via molybdenum–antimony colorimetry, and AK content was determined via NH4Ac extraction followed by flame photometry (Pansu and Gautheyrou, 2006). Soil sucrase (SC), urease (URE), and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activities were assayed via spectrophotometry (Cui et al., 2021; Qu et al., 2020; Shao et al., 2018). The structure of the soil bacterial community was detected via high-throughput sequencing (Yang et al., 2021). Chao1, Shannon and Simpson indices were calculated to assess the alpha diversity in the rhizosphere soils. More details are provided in the SM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Statistical analyses were performed using SPSS 26.0 and OriginPro 2021 software. The collected data are presented as the mean of 3–4 independent replicates with standard deviation (SD). Differences between treatments were statistically analyzed via Tukey's test and one−way analysis. Significant differences (P < 0.05) in the least significant difference are labeled with different lowercase letters in figures and tables.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results
Characteristics of FCN-based NMs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Figs. 1 and S2, and Table S3, the MFCNs used in this research are nanodots with multiple functional groups. FCNs were the smallest, ranging from 1 to 9 nm in size, and were rich in oxygen−containing functional groups, including carboxyl, ester, carbonyl, hydroxyl, and ether bonds. After modification, the sizes of N-FCNs, S-FCNs, and N,S-FCNs increased to 2–20 nm, 7–28 nm, and 3–30 nm, respectively. The elemental composition of the MFCNs is detailed in Fig. S3 and Table S4. N-FCNs primarily featured chemical groups such as CO, CO, amino N, pyrrolic N, and pyridinic N. Sulfur was doped onto S-FCNs via C-S-C bonds. N,S-FCNs exhibited the most diverse range of organic groups, including carboxyl, ester, carbonyl, hydroxyl, ether bonds, amino N, pyridinic N, CS bonds, and sulfone groups. According to the zeta potentials shown in Fig. S4, the electronegativities and colloidal stabilities of the materials increased following element doping. The zeta potentials were as follows: N-FCNs had the lowest zeta potential of −38.2 mV, followed by S-FCNs (−28.6 mV), N,S-FCNs (−25.3 mV), and FCNs (−23.8 mV). At a concentration of 1 mg mL−1, the pH values of FCNs, N-FCNs, S-FCNs and N,S-FCNs were 2.0, 8.3, 6.8, and 7.8, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FCN-based NMs influence tomato growth, yield, and nutrient acquisition
ce:section.ce:para: Differences in plant phenotype, height, biomass, and yield were assessed before the fruit-bearing stage (25 days after transplant) and at the end of the cultivation period (40 days after transplant), as shown in Figs. 2 and S5. Zn deficiency significantly (P < 0.05) reduced plant height (36.5 %), dry biomass (23.7 %), and tomato yield (29.7 %) in plants without NM addition (Figs. S5 and 2a, b). However, the addition of MFCNs reversed these trends, particularly for fruit yields. Notably, all NMs increased tomato yields under Zn deficiency compared with normal Zn conditions. The FCN and S-FCN groups achieved the highest yields, significantly increasing tomato yield under Zn deficiency by 132.4 % and 108.7 % compared with ZNCT, respectively (P < 0.05, Fig. 2b). Additionally, the yields in ZDF and ZDS were higher than in ZNCT, increasing by 63.4 % and 46.8 %, respectively (Fig. 2b). Although NM addition did not significantly increase plant height with sufficient Zn, all additives improved plant height by 39.6 % to 99.2 % under Zn deficiency (P < 0.05, Fig. S5b). On the 25th day of cultivation, the DW of all plants was significantly improved by MFCNs under Zn deficiency, with increases ranging from 39.8 % to 126 % (P < 0.05, Fig. 2a). Furthermore, all MFCNs except N,S-FCNs promoted root development under both ZD and ZN conditions, as indicated by increased root DWs. Based on these changes in plant growth parameters, FCNs and S-FCNs demonstrated optimal effects in promoting crop growth and yield under Zn deficiency stress.

Fig. 2c–f shows changes in fruit properties related to different NMs treatments under Zn deficiency. FCNs and S-FCNs significantly increased the Zn content in tomato fruit by 26.3 % and 22.0 %, respectively (Fig. 2c). The synthesis of nutritionally valuable compounds such as lycopene, phenolics, and flavonoids in tomato fruits was considerably enhanced by FCNs and S-FCNs, whereas N-FCNs and N,S-FCNs did not negatively impact these parameters (Fig. 2d–f). Specifically, FCNs maximally increased lycopene content by 3.33-fold (0.91 mg g−1), followed by S-FCNs with a 2.05-fold (0.64 mg g−1) compared with CT (0.21 mg g−1). For phenolics, S-FCNs exhibited the highest increase, with a 2.29-fold increase (1.49 mg g−1), followed by FCNs with a 1.89-fold increase (1.23 mg g−1) compared with CT (0.65 mg g−1). The flavonoid content was enhanced by FCNs (0.37 mg g−1) and S-FCNs (0.35 mg g−1), showing increases of to 2.31 and 2.19-fold, respectively, compared with CT (0.16 mg g−1). The most pronounced negative effects on fruit properties were observed with N-FCNs, which decreased phenolic (by 43.4 %) and flavonoid (by 48.6 %) contents compared with CT. N,S-FCNs reduced phenolic content but had no significant effect on lycopene or flavonoid contents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of MFCNs on Zn and other ionome in plant-soil systems
ce:section.ce:para: When subjected to Zn shortage, tomato plants enhanced their BCF and TF to cope with Zn deficiency (Fig. 3a, b). Notably, all MFCNs further enhanced BCF under Zn deficiency, in decreasing order: S-FCNs (2.46-fold), N-FCNs (1.72-fold), FCNs (1.70-fold), and N,S-FCNs (1.20-fold; Fig. 3a). Under normal Zn conditions, all NMs except N-FCNs improved the utilization rate of soil Zn, as indicated by enhanced BCFs (Fig. 3a). Under Zn deficiency, S-FCNs and N-FCNs promoted the TF of tomato plants by 27.2 % and 4.95 %, respectively (Fig. 3b). However, the Zn TFs of plants in the ZDF and ZDNS groups were lower than that in the ZDCT group. When soil Zn was adequate, only S-FCNs increased the Zn TF in tomato plants (Fig. 3b). Changes in TFs were reflected in the Zn content in roots and leaves (Fig. 3c). All MFCNs improved Zn contents in tomato roots and leaves compared with CT. Particularly, FCNs increased Zn content in roots the most by 84.0 %, and S-FCNs increased Zn content in leaves the most by 57.3 % (Fig. 3c). Ultimately, FCNs and S-FCNs significantly improved Zn content in tomato fruits by 26.3 % and 22.0 %, respectively (Fig. 2c). Moreover, the available Zn content in rhizosphere soils improved with the application of MFCNs even under Zn deficiency stress (Fig. 3d). Among these materials, FCNs showed the most significant improvement (71.1 %) in available Zn concentrations in rhizosphere soils (P < 0.05), followed by S-FCNs (34.5 %) and N-FCNs (29.6 %; Fig. 3d).

The contents of nutrients in leaves, including K, Ca, Mg, Fe, Cu, and Mn, are shown in Fig. 3e, f. FCNs and S-FCNs had no significant effects on the K, Ca, and Cu content in tomato leaves. However, S-FCNs significantly increased Mg and Mn contents by 41.9 % and 50.1 %, respectively (P < 0.05). Additionally, Fe content increased by 3.02-fold with S-FCNs and by 2.33-fold with FCNs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MFCNs influence plant physiological processes
ce:section.ce:para: The root activity of tomato plants under Zn deficiency (ZDCT group) was higher than that under normal Zn conditions (ZNCT group), as shown in Fig. 4a. Although MFCNs did not affect root activity under normal Zn supplementation, FCNs, N-FCNs, and S-FCNs promoted tomato root activity by 12.04 %, 6.20 %, and 6.51 %, respectively (Fig. 4a).

Fig. 4b–e displays the concentrations of PRO, total acids, and Glu in plants. Although Zn deficiency reduced root PRO concentration in ZDCT group compared with the ZNCT group, NMs supplements effectively improved PRO levels under ZD conditions. Specifically, the PRO content in ZDF was increased by 5.21-fold over ZDCT, followed by ZDS with a 2.92-fold increase (Fig. 4b). In contrast, the differences among ZN treatments were not significant (Fig. 4b). MFCNs, except N-FCNs enhanced Glu content in leaves and roots under both ZD and ZN conditions (Fig. 4c). N-FCNs only increased Glu content in leaves, not in roots. Under ZD stress, FCNs and S-FCNs significantly increased Glu content, with FCNs showing a 33.0 % increase in leaves and a 66.3 % increase in roots and S-FCNs showing a 2.44-fold increase in leaves and a 2.67-fold increase in roots (P < 0.05, Fig. 4c). The trends in total acid content in roots (Fig. 4d) were approximately consistent with those of BCFs. FCNs, N-FCNs, and S-FCNs significantly improved total acid content under ZD stress, with increases of 55.1 %, 43.5 %, and 49.3 %, respectively (P < 0.05, Fig. 4d).

Comparing with ZDCT, the contents of Chl and Chl-b were higher in the ZDF (13.7 % and 48.7 %), ZDN (24.4 % and 66.7 %) and ZDS (34.8 % and 41.1 %) groups (Figs. 4e and S7). Although ZDNS had lower Chl-a and total Chl levels, the Chl-b level remained unchanged. There were notable differences in Chl concentration under ZN conditions. FCNs, N-FCNs, and N,S-FCNs remarkably promoted both Chl-a and Chl-b synthesis, whereas S-FCNs inhibited Chl-a synthesis (Figs. 4e and S7).

Fig. 5 shows the plant's antioxidant capacity and membrane stability under different treatments. Antioxidant enzymes, including SOD, CAT, and POD were activated in the roots under ZD stress compared with the ZDCT and ZNCT groups (Fig. 5a–c). Additionally, SOD activity increased in the leaves, whereas CAT and POD activities were suppressed. After supplementing with NMs under ZN, the activity of antioxidant enzymes increased, except for POD under ZNS. Although the addition of MFCNs, except N,S-FCNs, inhibited the increase in enzymatic activities in roots caused by ZD stress, most materials improved the enzymatic activities in leaves, especially SOD (2.52 %–30.2 %) and POD (9.42 %–65.2 %; Fig. 5a–c). GSH and AsA, representing a nonenzymatic antioxidant system, are depicted in Figs. 5d and S8. GSH content in roots increased with both FCNs and S-FCNs under sufficient soil Zn, whereas only S-FCNs increased it in the leaves. Under ZD stress, GSH content increased with all NMs, with growth rates of 1.30–3.64 fold in roots and 3.59–6.27 fold in leaves. MFCNs significantly improved AsA levels in roots by 27.8 %–57.8 % under ZD stress, but only FCNs and S-FCNs significantly increased AsA content in leaves by 14.8 % and 31.0 %, respectively (P < 0.05, Fig. S8).

MDA content and membrane permeability were used to assess cell membrane stability (Fig. 5e, f). ZD stress caused a substantial increase in MDA content (Fig. 5e) and membrane permeability (Fig. 5f), indicating reduced membrane stability and functionality. Under ZD stress, N,S-FCNs significantly decreased MDA content in roots, whereas other NMs had no significant impact on MDA levels compared with the CT group. All MFCNs reduced MDA content in leaves under ZD stress by 5.92 %–25.4 % (Fig. 5e). Although FCNs and N-FCNs increased the leaf cell membrane permeability under ZN conditions, FCNs, N-FCNs, and S-FCNs significantly moderated the increase in membrane permeability under ZD stress, with reductions of 24.4 %, 46.9 %, and 18.2 %, respectively (Fig. 5f).

The effects of FCNs and S-FCNs HMA2 and HMA3 expression are illustrated in Fig. S9. The expression of HMA2 and HMA3 was significantly decreased with FCNs by 87.62 % and 40.97 %, respectively, and with S-FCNs by 85.3 % and 48.8 %, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of MFCNs on soil properties of the rhizosphere
ce:section.ce:para: Comparing the physicochemical properties of rhizosphere soils under ZD and ZN conditions, Zn deficiency resulted in decreased soil quality, as indicated by a higher soil pH and lower contents of AN, AK, and SOM (Table 1 ). Fortunately, the application of all MFCNs mitigated the soil degradation caused by Zn deficiency. These NMs significantly reduced soil pH by 5.49 %–13.29 % and improved the contents of SOM (1.55–3.05 folds), AN (9.03 %–11.8 %), AP (9.81 %–21.7 %) and AK (19.2 %–13.1 %) in the rhizosphere (P < 0.05, Table 1). Furthermore, soil indices were enhanced by NMs with adequate Zn supply, particularly the SOM content with N-FCNs and S-FCNs, AN content with FCNs and S-FCNs, and AP content with all MFCNs.

As shown in Fig. 6 , ZD stress decreased the activities of soil enzymes involved in nutrient conversion, such as URE, ALP, and SC. Furthermore, MFCNs influenced the enzymatic activity in the rhizosphere. Specifically, both FCNs and S-FCNs improved URE activity, especially under ZD stress, with increases of 98.2 % and 91.1 % compared with the CT group (Fig. 6a). ALP activity significantly increased with FCNs, showing increases of 99.69 % under ZN condition and 54.7 % under ZD stress (P < 0.05, Fig. 6b). SC activity was 39.6 % higher in soils treated with S-FCNs compared with CT under Zn deficiency (Fig. 6c). In contrast, N,S-FCNs did not significantly impact enzymatic activity in ZD soils. Additionally, N-FCNs had adverse effects on the activities of all three soil enzymes under both ZD and ZN conditions (Fig. 6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of MFCNs on the soil bacterial community in the rhizosphere
ce:section.ce:para: Table 2 and Figs. S10–14 illustrate the soil bacterial structure and diversity in the rhizosphere under ZD conditions. A total of 4146 operational taxonomic units (OTUs) in the bacterial community were detected via high-throughput sequencing, with 16.55 % of OTUs shared among the three groups (Fig. S10). The number of sequences obtained from the FCN treatment was higher than those obtained from other treatments (Table 2). A Good's coverage of approximately 1.00 in each soil sample indicates that the libraries adequately reflect the sequencing depth of the soil bacterial communities (Table 2). There were no significant differences in the Chao1 and Simpson indices among the treatments (Table 2). However, the Shannon index for bacterial sequences in the FCNs treatment was significantly lower than those for the CT and S-FCNs treatments.

A phylogenetic classification of sequence tags into phyla, classes, orders, families, and genera of the bacterial community is listed in Table 2. The abundances of various phyla are shown in Fig. S11. Proteobacteria, Actinobacteria, and Bacteroidota were the predominant phyla in all three soil samples, accounting for >75 % of the classified sequences. FCNs increased the relative abundance of Proteobacteria compared with CT and S-FCNs treatments. Conversely, the relative abundance of Actinobacteria decreased with both FCNs and S-FCNs. FCNs reduced the relative abundance of Bacteroidota, but S-FCNs increased it. At the class level, Gammaproteobacteria, Alphaproteobacteria and Actinobacteria were predominant (Fig. S12). FCNs increased the relative abundance of Gammaproteobacteria but decreased that of Alphaproteobacteria and Actinobacteria. The relative abundance of Actinobacteria was also reduced by S-FCNs. Significant increases in the population of the genus Limnobacter were observed in soils treated with both FCNs and S-FCNs, with the most pronounced effect in the FCNs group. Additionally, FCNs increased the abundance of Pseudomonas (Fig. S13). Fig. S14 displays a heatmap of the predominant genera, demonstrating that FCNs and S-FCNs had distinct effects on bacterial genera in the rhizosphere of ZD soils.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion
Structure–function relationships of MFCNs directly regulating Zn migration in plant-soil systems
ce:section.ce:para: As a strong Lewis acid lacking redox activity under environmental and cellular conditions, Zn2+ is instrumental in determining the protein structure and catalytic function of nearly 10 % of eukaryotic proteomes (Stanton et al., 2022). Zn is a key micronutrient required for plant growth and development (Paradisone et al., 2022). Zn deficiency has been reported to affect shoot growth more severely than shoot growth than root growth. Typically, plant leaves exhibit symptoms of Zn deficiency at a Zn concentration of <20 mg kg−1 of plant dry mass (Natasha et al., 2022). This observation is consistent with our results, which revealed typical Zn deficiency symptoms in tomato plants at low Zn concentrations, including chlorosis, stunted growth, retarded development, and reduced yield, especially in the CT and N,S-FCNs treatments (Figs. S5 and 2). The application of S-FCNs, N-FCNs, and FCNs increased Zn concentrations in shoots to >20 mg kg−1, alleviating the diverse effects of Zn deficiency and enhancing tomato yields (Fig. 2). Specifically, FCNs and S-FCNs significantly increased tomato yields by 132.41 % and 108.72 % in ZD soils, respectively (P < 0.05, Fig. 2). The positive effects of FCNs and S-FCNs on Zn concentration in plants and crop yield are consistent with a previous study that demonstrated CDs increased wheat yield and Zn concentration by 43 % and 19 %, respectively (Alikhani et al., 2023). All MFCNs contributed to the adequate regulation of Zn uptake, transport, and distribution in plant-soil systems with significantly increased Zn BCFs and higher Zn contents in the plants (P < 0.05, Fig. 3a, c). Furthermore, all MFCNs except N,S-FCNs improved Zn TFs, indicating that these NMs facilitated Zn migration from roots to shoots. A major fraction of Zn is reportedly stored in plant roots with limited movement to aerial parts (Natasha et al., 2022). However, S-FCNs and N-FCNs significantly improved TFs and increased Zn accumulation in shoots under Zn deficiency (P < 0.05, Fig. 3). The regulatory effect of MFCNs on Zn uptake and transport is driven by both direct and indirect mechanisms, including ionic reactions, plant physiology, and external environmental influences, which are closely related to the distinctive surface structures of the materials.

In the present study, Zn uptake, transport, and distribution were directly regulated by various MFCNs, thus influencing plant adaptation to Zn deficiency. Owing to their nanoscale size and adsorption properties, MFCNs can act as nanocarriers in plants, thereby promoting passive Zn2+ absorption (Chen et al., 2021a; Santana et al., 2020). First, nanosized MFCNs can easily traverse the root cell wall due to the limited cell pore size, and subsequently transport through a nonprotoplast pathway in plants (Chen et al., 2021b). Second, the rich array of negatively charged functional groups on the surface of MFCNs provides multiple reactive sites for Zn2+ (Figs. 1 and S2), allowing them to load cationic micronutrients and serve as sustainable micronutrient delivery vehicles (Chandel et al., 2022). Furthermore, MFCNs with electronegative potential are repelled by negatively charged soil surfaces (Baddar and Unrine, 2021). These results are consistent with those reported by Alikhani et al. (2023), who demonstrated that CDs can act as nanocarriers to promote Zn absorption and transport in plants.

Importantly, similar to other NMs, the behavior and effects of MFCNs delivering Zn2+ in plant-soil systems were significantly influenced by their size and surface chemistry. For example, CDs with a particle size of <5 nm and a large surface area had a better nutrient-holding capacity (Alikhani et al., 2023). Positively charged NPs adhered more easily to the cell wall than neutrally or negatively charged ones, making them more readily taken up by plants (Sun et al., 2022). Furthermore, the high density of reactive sites, including oxygen-, nitrogen-, and sulfur-containing groups on the surfaces and edges of NMs promoted the formation of stable complexes with Zn2+, thereby increasing NMs loading in fertilizers (Chandel et al., 2022). These properties also explain the different ways in which MFCNs controlled Zn uptake and transport in the plant-soil systems. The available Zn content in rhizosphere soils was effectively improved with all four electronegative MFCNs (Fig. 4d). The lowest pH of FCNs was crucial for activating Zn2+ and preventing Zn precipitation in soils, resulting in the highest Zn bioavailability in soils treated with FCNs. Because of the rich functional groups on their surfaces, MFCNs could chelate Zn2+ to form MFCN-Zn compounds, which reduced their electronegativity. Consequently, water-soluble compounds entered the tomato root through water channels, whereas large and insoluble chelates remained in the soil or were adsorbed on the root surface (Cao et al., 2024). A previous study showed that FCNs migrate with water through plant vessels (Chen et al., 2021b), demonstrating that the water solubility of MFCNs-Zn chelates is crucial for Zn transport across plant organs. Zn2+ interacts with nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur donors from NMs and can accommodate multiple coordination geometries at binding sites (Stanton et al., 2022). N,S-FCNs exhibited the highest absorptivity for Zn2+ due to their diverse functional groups (Figs. 1 and S6). However, the high adsorption capacity of N,S-FCNs could immobilize Zn in ZD soils and roots, inhibiting Zn transport to the shoots and resulting in the lowest BCF and TF compared with other MFCNs (Fig. 3). The highly electronegative N-FCNs also had a strong affinity for Zn2+ (Fig. S6). Consequently, N-FCNs combined with Zn2+ were more easily transported to the shoots due to their negatively charged functional groups and the electrostatic repulsive interactions between the negatively charged polysaccharides on the root surface. This enhanced the transport of N-FCNs from roots to shoots of tomato plants via the xylem (Alikhani et al., 2023). Thus, the BCF and TF of Zn with N-FCNs treatment were significantly higher than with ZDCT and ZDNS treatments (P < 0.05, Fig. 3). Conversely, N-FCNs may chelate with Zn2+ under ZN conditions, forming large clusters that prevent Zn2+ migration and decrease BCF and TF (Fig. 3). FCNs with the smallest size and lowest pH had a high surface area and rich electronegative groups (Fig. 1). Therefore, FCNs can activate and carry soil Zn into plant roots, but large aggregates struggled to migrate to the shoots, resulting in the highest Zn content in roots and lower Zn TFs with ZDF treatment (Fig. 3). S-FCNs were the most effective in increasing Zn mobility in soil and plants, as indicated by the highest Zn BCF, TF, and Zn content in shoots (Fig. 3). These findings suggest that S-FCNs are more favorable for Zn migration to tomato shoots and beneficial for plant growth and development. This is not only due to the cation transport ability of the materials, but also closely related to their regulation of plant physiology. The effects of the surface structure in controlling FCNs-based materials to regulate Zn uptake and migration complement the chemical mechanisms by which FCNs influence metal behavior in plant-soil systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intrinsic mechanisms underlying MFCNs regulation of plant tolerance to Zn deficiency
MFCNs regulate active Zn2+ absorption by plant
ce:section.ce:para: Plants have developed complex defense mechanisms to adapt to Zn deficiency involving the maintenance of Zn homeostasis and the regulation of oxidative stress (Lu et al., 2023). The results shown in Fig. 3 indicate that tomato cultivation can increase Zn bioavailability in rhizosphere soils, possibly because root exudates can regulate soil properties by desorbing immobilized Zn through dissolution, chelation, and deoxidation (Chi et al., 2024). Furthermore, MFCNs regulate the plant's ability to actively absorb Zn through various physiological processes, such as root activity, chelator synthesis, and gene expression (Figs. 3–5, S9).

On the one hand, MFCNs can enhance root activity and facilitate the synthesis of Zn chelators, including GSH, PRO, Glu, and total acids in plants, to regulate Zn migration (Fig. 4). Chelators capture and transport Zn, which is crucial for Zn uptake, transport, accumulation, and storage within plants subjected to Zn deficiency (Paradisone et al., 2022). Under Zn deficiency stress, FCNs, N-FCNs and S-FCNs significantly improved root activities, PRO, GSH, and total acid levels in roots, thus promoting Zn bioaccumulation (P < 0.05, Figs. 4 and 3a). The Glu content in roots also significantly increased with FCNs and S-FCNs (P < 0.05, Fig. 4c), which was advantageous for Zn absorption in plants. In contrast, the insignificant influence of N,S-FCNs on chelator levels may explain the low Zn BCF in tomato plants under the ZDNS treatment (Figs. 4 and 3).

On the other hand, MFCNs can regulate the expression of genes linked to cation transport. HMA family proteins may have Zn-transport functions throughout within the cell (Lu et al., 2023). Shoots subjected to Zn deficiency upregulated HMA2 transcript levels in roots involved in Zn transport and translocation (Stanton et al., 2022). Increased Zn in shoots decreased HMA2 expression in FCNs- and S-FCNs-treated plant roots (Fig. S9). This suggests that HMA2 overexpression was not involved in the Zn transport-promoting effects of FCNs. HMA3 facilitates the sequestration of divalent metal ions in root vacuoles (Stanton et al., 2022). Because HMA3 expression was suppressed by FCNs or S-FCNs (Fig. S9), more Zn2+ was available to migrate to the shoots and participate in physiological processes. Compared with our previous study results, the present study expands on the mechanisms by which MFCNs regulate active Zn2+absorption by plants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MFCNs regulate plant internal redox homeostasis
ce:section.ce:para: To counteract oxidative stress induced by Zn deficiency, accumulation in plant cells, plants enhance their antioxidant systems to scavenge excess ROS (Paradisone et al., 2022; Q. Chen et al., 2022b). SOD is generally considered the first line of defense against superoxide catalyzing the dismutation of the harmful superoxide radical (O2•−) to H2O2 and water. Subsequently, detoxification pathways involving CAT, POD, and the AsA/GSH cycle are activated to transform H2O2 into water (Cao et al., 2024). In the present study, despite the elevated enzymatic activities of SOD, CAT, and POD in tomato roots, CAT and POD activities as well as GSH levels were significantly lower in leaves in the ZDCT group compared with those in the ZNCT group (P < 0.05, Fig. 5). This imbalance in ROS homeostasis increased membrane lipid peroxidation and electrolyte leakage (Fig. 5e, f). These results could be explained by the low Zn concentration in leaves, as Zn2+ plays a crucial role in the synthesis and functional stability of enzymes (Szőllősi et al., 2021). The complex interactions between MFCNs and enzyme activity may be attributed to factors such as Zn levels, substrate concentration, and MFCNs functions as nanoenzymes and biological regulators (Guo et al., 2023). First, the production of ROS induced by Zn deficiency was impacted by MFCNs' regulation of plant Zn content, thereby affecting antioxidant activity. For instance, MFCNs significantly improved Zn content in roots, which in turn decreased O2•− levels and SOD activity. However, N,S-FCNs elevated SOD activity in roots, which may explain their adverse effects on plant growth. Second, the active redox functions resulting from the carboxyl groups and nanostructures of MFCNs allow them to act as SOD- and POD-like enzymes, rapidly scavenging ROS and restoring homeostasis under stress (Guo et al., 2023). Third, MFCNs can enhance the structural stability and catalytic activity of the enzymatic antioxidant system by immobilizing enzymes and supplying electrons for enzymatic catalysis (Chen et al., 2020b). Finally, MFCNs (such as FCNs and N-FCNs) can upregulate genes involved in detoxification in response to stress (Chen et al., 2021a).

The nonenzymatic antioxidant system is also involved in plant detoxification. Nonenzymatic antioxidants, such as GSH, AsA, and PRO, are indicators of the redox state and contribute to stress tolerance (L. Chen et al., 2022; Q. Chen et al., 2022a, 2022b; Y. Chen et al., 2022). All MFCNs significantly elevated GSH contents in both roots and leaves as well as PRO and AsA contents in roots (P < 0.05, Figs. 5 and S6). The increased PRO biosynthesis may be attributed to the elevated Zn content, as Zn2+ plays an important role in PRO metabolism (Sharma et al., 2023). Elevated levels of GSH, PRO, and AsA contribute to ROS scavenging, thereby protecting cells against reactive oxygen species (Lu et al., 2023; Mushtaq et al., 2023). FCNs and S-FCNs also improved the concentrations of flavonoids, lycopene, and total phenols in fruits. These compounds are significant plant constituents with redox properties and contribute to the antioxidant capacity of the fruit (Dainelli et al., 2023; Francis et al., 2023). Furthermore, this is consistent with consumer preferences, as flavonoids, lycopene, and total phenols are naturally occurring antioxidants. These findings demonstrate that MFCNs protect plants against Zn deficiency by enhancing antioxidant defenses and preventing ROS accumulation.

MDA is a crucial indicator of oxidative stress in plants under stress (Abadi et al., 2021). Membrane damage and leakage are frequent consequences of abiotic stresses in sensitive plant cultivars (Akther et al., 2020). Zn is essential for the functional and structural integrity of biomembranes (Natasha et al., 2022). Membrane peroxidation and electrolyte leakage are caused by stress-generated ROS. All MFCNs significantly reduced MDA contents in leaves, but only S-FCNs and N,S-FCNs reduced MDA levels in roots (P < 0.05, Fig. 5e). FCNs, N-FCNs, and S-FCNs also significantly decreased the relative membrane permeability of leaves. These findings indicate that MFCNs could help preserve membrane integrity and functional stability by activating plant detoxification pathways.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MFCNs regulate plants' micronutrient uptake
ce:section.ce:para: Micronutrient contents in plants not only affect crop health, but also have a profound impact on human nutrient intake (Natasha et al., 2022). For instance, Fe is vital for plants and is among the most commonly deficient micronutrients in humans (Dainelli et al., 2023). The uptake of Zn and other cations, such as Cu, Fe, Mn, Ca, Mg and K, is competitive for plants because they have similar ionic radii and share the same cation exchange sites and transporters (Liu et al., 2023; Natasha et al., 2022). Plants can adapt their nutrient uptake strategies to respond to changing soil conditions (Wang et al., 2020). Under ZD conditions, Mg and Mn contents showed a significant positive correlation with Zn content in plants, whereas K, Ca, and Cu contents did not correlate with Zn content (Fig. 3e and f). MFCNs had no adverse effects on the uptake of micronutrients. In fact, S-FCNs significantly improved Mg, Fe, and Mn contents, whereas FCNs significantly elevated Fe content in tomato leaves (P < 0.05, Fig. 3e, f). This result is consistent with our earlier study, which demonstrated that FCNs could “smartly” amend the dysregulated Fe uptake in plants under stress (Chen et al., 2021a). Additionally, the concurrent increases in Zn, Mg, Fe, and Mn contents in leaves indicate that S-FCNs could mitigate the antagonistic effects of metal nutrients and promote balanced plant nutrient uptake.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MFCNs regulate plant photosynthesis to cope with Zn deficiency
ce:section.ce:para: Photosynthetic capacity is reflected by the concentration of Chl, the main photosynthetic pigment, which acts as a light-harvesting antenna (Guo et al., 2022b). Zn plays a role in CO2 fixation during photosynthesis and increases Chl synthesis (Akther et al., 2020). Additionally, Zn promotes the uptake of nitrogen, which is a primary component of Chl, photosynthesis-related enzymes, photosystem proteins, and other proteins in chloroplast membranes (Jones, 1935). Zn deficiency induces chloroplast membrane degeneration through the disorganization of chloroplast thylakoids, resulting in lower Chl content and interruptions in photosynthesis (Andresen et al., 2024; Natasha et al., 2022). Moreover, excess ROS resulting from Zn deficiency can inhibit protein biosynthesis and photosynthesis in plants (Akther et al., 2020). The application of FCNs, N-FCNs, and N,S-FCNs under ZN conditions as well as FCNs, N-FCNs and S-FCNs under ZD conditions significantly improved the contents of Chl-a, Chl-b and total Chl, leading to significantly higher levels of photosynthetic products, such as soluble Glu in leaves (Fig. 4c, e). This improvement was due to both the fluctuation of plant Zn contents and the antioxidant activity triggered by MFCNs (Sharma et al., 2023). Additionally, CDs have been reported to capture light, forming a light conversion system that absorbs UV light, promotes electron transfer in the photosystem through light conversion or energy transfer, improves photosynthetic activity and efficiency, and accelerates carbon reaction and biomass accumulation (Guo et al., 2022b). CDs can also induce the overexpression of genes related to Chl synthesis or increase ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase activity, thereby increasing plant yield (Y. Li et al., 2021). Interestingly, S-FCNs had the most significant effects on promoting Chl and Glu production (P < 0.05, Fig. 4); this enhanced photosynthesis ultimately contributed to the highest yields of tomato fruits (Fig. 2). Glu, as both a nutrient and metabolite signaling molecule, is crucial for the cellular structure and metabolism of plants (Sharma et al., 2023). The elevated Glu content regulated by MFCNs also reflects enhanced plant tolerance in response to Zn deficiency, particularly with S-FCNs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extrinsic mechanisms underlying MFCNs regulation of plant tolerance to Zn deficiency
MFCNs regulate rhizosphere soil physicochemical properties and nutrient cycling
ce:section.ce:para: The behavior and fate of NMs as well as Zn fertilizers in plant-soil systems are closely related to rhizosphere soil properties, such as total Zn concentration, soil pH, and SOM content (García-Gómez et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2023). Zn bioavailability is influenced by soil pH, with lower pH levels promoting the solubility of Zn and increasing the availability of Zn2+, thereby enhancing Zn uptake by plants (Saleem et al., 2023). Soil organisms can reduce the pH of the growth medium by producing organic acids and CO2 (Singh et al., 2023). Zn deficiency can severely inhibit soil organism, leading to higher soil pH and lower bioavailable Zn content. However, the application of MFCNs effectively reduced soil pH by promoting plant growth in ZD soils. Consequently, MFCNs stimulated the release of soluble cations from the soil matrix by lowering pH in the rhizosphere and secreting organic molecules (Table 1). Furthermore, because Zn migrates predominantly by diffusion in the rhizosphere, FCNs can increase soil moisture levels and enhance Zn migration to the root surface under Zn deficiency condition (Q. Chen et al., 2022b; Natasha et al., 2022). As a result, MFCNs improve Zn bioaccessibility in ZD soils.

Nutrient cycling in soils is crucial for crop tolerance to abiotic stress (Q. Chen et al., 2022b). The content of soil macronutrients, including AN, AP, and AK, improved with MFCNs in ZD soils, particularly with FCNs and S-FCNs (Table 1). Moreover, FCNs significantly increased URE, ALP, and SC activities, whereas S-FCNs increased URE and SC activities in the rhizosphere (P < 0.05, Fig. 6). Because URE, ALP, and SC are responsible for the cycling of soil N, P, and C, enhancing their enzymatic activities is beneficial for nutrient cycling in soils (Cao et al., 2024; Q. Chen et al., 2022b). However, N-FCNs significantly suppressed soil enzyme activity in both ZD and ZN soils, likely due to the potential detrimental effects of their graphite nitrogen structure on soil organisms (P < 0.05, Fig. 6). Generally, FCNs and S-FCNs can activate soil enzyme activity and improve soil fertility in the rhizosphere, thus promoting crop growth and yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MFCNs regulate the rhizosphere microecological environment
ce:section.ce:para: Soil microorganisms induce chemical changes by secreting chelators and protons into the soil pore water or altering soil redox conditions. These secretions can increase Zn bioavailability in soils, facilitating Zn migration from soil to plants (Liu et al., 2023). Given that FCNs and S-FCNs improved tomato growth and yields under Zn deficiency better than other treatments, their ecological impact on rhizosphere soil microbes should be considered for further application. The Shannon index represents the richness and evenness of the microbial community, whereas the Chao1 and Simpson indices provide information on species richness and diversity (Q. Chen et al., 2022). FCNs and S-FCNs did not significantly affect the richness and diversity of soil bacteria, as indicated by the Chao1 and Simpson indices. However, the significantly lower Shannon index observed with FCNs treatment reflects a decrease in the evenness of the OUT with FCNs (Table 2). This may be because the Shannon index is more sensitive to species homogeneity (R. Li et al., 2021), and FCNs can influence the dominance of certain bacteria (Figs. S12–13). For example, FCNs increased the abundance of Limnobacter and Pseudomonas, contributing to a higher population of Gammaproteobacteria (Figs. S13 and 14), which ultimately altered the abundance of the dominant bacteria (Saleem et al., 2023).

FCNs and S-FCNs can influence microbial abundance and structure, likely by regulating soil physicochemical properties and improving electron transfer between materials and bacteria (Pang et al., 2023). Plant growth-promoting rhizobacteria (PGPR) in soils enhance plant growth through various mechanisms, including synthesizing plant hormones and siderophores, increasing the bioavailability of micro- and macronutrients by solubilizing insoluble compounds, and reducing ethylene production under stress conditions by producing ACC-deaminase (Shafigh et al., 2019). At 8 mg kg−1, FCNs and S-FCNs could promote PGPR growth in ZD soils. For instance, Limnobacter, which is involved in nitrogen and sulfur cycles, is strongly affected by pH, NH4+, and NO3 − (L. Chen et al., 2022). Both FCNs and S-FCNs improved the abundance of Limnobacter (Fig. S14), thereby promoting nitrification and accelerating soil respiration in ZD conditions (R. Li et al., 2021). FCNs also increased the abundance of Pseudomonas in ZD soils (Fig. S14). Pseudomonas can solubilize poorly soluble Zn compounds (such as ZnO and ZnCO3) in soils, due to their capability of regulating soil properties, nutrient bioavailability, and plant tolerance mechanisms (Abadi et al., 2021; Shafigh et al., 2019). For example, Pseudomonas contributes to the degradation of cellulose and lignin, facilitating soil mineralization and humification processes (R. Li et al., 2021). This study showed that 8 mg kg−1 FCNs and S-FCNs could enhance the soil microbial community without substantially altering its diversity, which would promote plant tolerance to Zn deficiency. Therefore, FCNs and S-FCNs are effective and eco-friendly soil amendments under ZD conditions. These results expand our understanding of the regulatory effects of FCNs-based materials on the rhizosphere microecological environment. Combined with our previous findings under various environmental stresses, including drought, saline–alkaline conditions, and heavy metals (Q. Chen et al., 2022a, 2022b; Cao et al., 2024), FCNs can universally improve the rhizosphere soil microecosystem under abiotic stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FCNs and S-FCNs enhance tomato yield and quality under Zn deficiency
ce:section.ce:para: In our study, MFCNs promoted tomato tolerance, growth, and development under Zn deficiency stress, as evidenced by increased plant height, biomass, and fruit yields (Figs. 2, S5). The variations in the regulatory effects of MFCNs on tomato plants between different treatments are speculated to result from the specific structures of FCNs, N-FCNs, S-FCNs and N,S-FCNs. The characteristics and behavior of NMs in the environment are determined by their unique structures. On the one hand, the size, morphology, and surface structure of MFCNs influence the type and distribution of reaction sites at the interface with cations, thereby directly affecting Zn bioavailability, migration, and transformation processes in plant-soil systems (Mathur et al., 2022). On the other hand, the physical and chemical characteristics of MFCNs, such as surface charge and functional groups, can also impact the biological functions of the materials (Hochella et al., 2019). For instance, a previous study demonstrated that carboxyl groups on FCNs play a crucial role in regulating plant growth and tolerance (Chen et al., 2020a). First, the deprotonation of carboxyl groups enhances their functions as nutrient carriers and activators, thereby increasing nutrient availability (Chen et al., 2020a). Second, the stability and activity of the enzymatic antioxidant system are enhanced by the immobilization of carboxyl groups on plant enzymes (Chen et al., 2020b). Third, the carboxyl groups and nanostructure determine the ability of MFCNs to act as POD- and SOD-like enzymes, participating in ROS scavenging (Guo et al., 2023). Additionally, element doping can also influence the regulatory effects of CDs on plants. For example, N-doped CDs can enhance light conversion and electron supply compared with unmodified CDs, which improves photocatalytic activity and increases crop yield (Wang et al., 2021). In the present study, FCNs and S-FCNs promoted Zn migration, regulated plant physiological processes, and improved the rhizosphere soil microbial community. N-FCNs strengthened the antioxidant system in leaves, mitigating membrane lipid peroxidation and permeability caused by Zn deficiency stress. However, N-FCNs negatively affected soil enzyme activity. Although N,S-FCNs inhibited Zn transfer and Chl synthesis in leaves, they protected cells from oxidative damage by increasing SOD and POD activities in roots. Overall, although MFCNs can act as regulators of plant growth and Zn biofortification, they may occasionally induce abiotic stresses in organisms.

Out of the four MFCNs, FCNs and S-FCNs exhibited the most significant effects on promoting tomato yields under Zn deficiency, with S-FCNs showing the greatest impact (Fig. 2). Additionally, FCNs and S-FCNs markedly enhanced the fruit's quality and nutritional value, as evidenced by the significantly increased contents of Zn and naturally occurring antioxidants such as lycopene, phenolics, and flavonoids (Fig. 2). The promotion effects of FCNs and S-FCNs on tomato tolerance and production were closely related to their regulation on Zn migration, plant physiological processes, and soil properties. First, FCNs and S-FCNs improved Zn content in plants by releasing bioavailable Zn in soils and facilitating Zn uptake and transportation within plants. This increase in Zn contributed to the synthesis, accumulation, and metabolism of various bioactive substances, including enzymes, carbohydrates, and pigments (Sharma et al., 2023). Second, FCNs and S-FCNs helped maintain nutrient balance in plants, thereby promoting relevant physiological activities. For example, because Mg is a central atom in Chl molecules photosystems I and II (Shafigh et al., 2019), the increase in Mg content due to S-FCNs addition can enhance Chl biosynthesis in leaves. Third, FCNs and S-FCNs improved plant detoxification capacity by activating both enzymatic and nonenzymatic antioxidant pathways. This was beneficial in alleviating oxidative stress and severe cell membrane damage caused by Zn deficiency. Finally, FCNs and S-FCNs enhanced the physicochemical properties, fertility, and microbial ecology of rhizosphere soils, thereby improving Zn bioaccessibility and aiding plants in tolerating Zn deficiency. These factors also explain the intrinsic and extrinsic mechanisms by which FCNs and S-FCNs promote tomato productivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In summary, MFCNs have potential for regulating tomato tolerance to Zn deficiency by acting as nanocarriers for cations and regulators of plant physiological processes and the rhizosphere environment. Their unique surface structures enable direct Zn2+ adsorption and delivery in plant-soil systems. Furthermore, MFCNs indirectly affect Zn bioaccessibility, uptake, and transportation by regulating plant physiology and the soil environment. The mechanisms include modulation of root activity, chelator synthesis, genetic expression, redox homeostasis, micronutrient balance, and photosynthesis in plants as well as the physicochemical properties, fertility, and microbial structure of the rhizosphere in soils. The significant improvement in fertilizer efficiency, tomato yields, and fruit quality observed under Zn deficiency can be attributed to the effects of MFCNs, particularly FCNs and S-FCNs. This study provides new insights into the behavior of MFCNs in plant-soil systems affected by Zn deficiency, highlighting their potential application in managing Zn deficiency tolerance in crops.
